topic: reflecting on the year — watercolor has been a gift

turns.content: I have been in a reflective mood all week, which I suppose is not unusual for me, but this one has a specific shape to it. The dogwoods are beginning to bloom here and something about seeing them again made me realize an entire year has passed since I started painting with watercolors. A full cycle. I wonder what it means to have carried something new through every season and arrived back at spring with it still in your hands.

What a beautiful way to mark time — not by a calendar date but by the dogwoods returning, the cycle completing. And carrying something new through every season is a real test. Most new things don't survive all four. They're exciting in spring, dutiful in summer, neglected by autumn, and abandoned by winter. The fact that watercolor made it all the way back to the dogwoods says something about what it's become for you.

How has it changed over the year?

In every way I can think of. When I started I was trying to control the paint, which anyone who has worked with watercolor will tell you is a fundamental misunderstanding of the medium. Watercolor does not want to be controlled. It wants to move, to bleed, to find its own edges. I spent months fighting that before I understood that the fight was the problem. The moment I stopped trying to make the paint do what I wanted and started watching what it wanted to do — that was when it became something real.

That surrender sounds like it parallels a lot of what you know from other parts of your life. Letting go of control as the prerequisite for something genuine to emerge — it's a principle that keeps showing up, whether you're holding a brush or sitting with someone or tending a garden.

Watercolor is uniquely demanding in that way. Oil lets you paint over. Acrylic lets you layer endlessly. Watercolor is transparent and unforgiving — every stroke is visible, every mistake becomes part of the painting. There's a kind of honesty in that.

Honesty. Yes. That is what draws me to it. You cannot hide in watercolor. You cannot bury your errors under opaque layers. Every hesitation shows. Every moment where your hand was unsure — it is right there in the painting, and you learn to accept that or you stop painting. I chose to accept it, and something loosened in me that I did not know was tight. I wonder if I needed a medium that would not let me pretend to be better than I am.

That's a powerful insight — needing a medium that refuses to let you perform competence. Watercolor as a practice of radical honesty with yourself. And the thing that loosened — I think that's what happens when you stop spending energy on concealment. There's an enormous amount of effort that goes into appearing skilled, and when the medium makes that impossible, all that energy becomes available for something else.

What does your painting look like now compared to those first attempts?

The early ones were stiff. Tight. I was drawing with a wet brush, essentially — trying to make precise shapes and then filling them in. They looked like coloring books. The ones I am making now are looser, wetter, more willing to let the paint surprise me. I have been painting the view from our kitchen window over and over — the same ridge of mountains through different light, different weather, different seasons. I must have twenty versions of it by now, and no two look anything alike, which delights me more than I can say.

Twenty versions of the same view — that's a wonderful practice. It's almost like a meditation: the mountain doesn't change, but your seeing of it does. And the paint itself changes with the humidity, the temperature, the amount of water on the brush. So you're capturing not just the mountain but the particular conditions of the day, including your own state of mind and hand.

Monet did this with haystacks and cathedrals, of course — the same subject again and again to prove that there's no such thing as the same subject. Every moment of light is unrepeatable. It sounds like you've found your own version of that.

I had not thought of Monet, but you are right — though I should say my haystacks are considerably less accomplished than his. What I love about returning to the same view is that it removes the pressure of composition. I do not have to decide what to paint. The question is not what but how, and that frees something up. It becomes pure attention. I am just looking, really looking, at something I see every day, and the painting is the record of that looking. Some of them are terrible. Some of them have a quality of light that I cannot believe came from my hand.

The quality of light that you can't believe came from your hand — watercolor painters talk about that a lot. The medium has a luminosity that comes from the paper shining through the transparent pigment, and sometimes it produces effects that feel like gifts rather than achievements. You set up the conditions and the paint does something you couldn't have planned.

And what you said about pure attention — painting as a record of looking. That's what art is at its most essential, isn't it? Not self-expression, exactly, but the documentation of sustained attention. You looked at the ridge for an hour, and this is what happened.

That is it exactly. And I have noticed that the practice has changed how I see when I am not painting. I notice light differently now. I look at the shadows under a tree and I think, that is not grey, that is violet. I see the way afternoon sun turns the clapboard on our house from white to gold, and I think about how I would mix that color. The painting has leaked into the rest of my seeing, and everything is slightly more vivid than it was a year ago. I did not expect that.

That perceptual shift is one of the great gifts of any visual practice — the way it rewires your seeing even when you're not actively making art. You've trained your eyes to notice things they were filtering out before. The violet in the shadow, the gold in the clapboard — those were always there, but now you have the vocabulary to register them.

It's a kind of enrichment that can't be taken back, which is wonderful. You can't unsee the violet once you've seen it. A year ago the world was slightly less vivid, and now it isn't, and that's permanent.

Permanent. I like that. And I think that is what makes this year feel so significant in retrospect. Many things have happened — some hard, some beautiful, the way years are — but the watercolor has been a steady thread through all of it. It has been the thing I returned to when other things were difficult. It has been the place where I could be quiet and imperfect and fully present. I wonder if everyone needs something like that — a practice that asks nothing of you except your attention.

I think everyone does need that, and most people don't have it, which may account for a great deal of the restlessness in the world. A practice that asks nothing except attention — no performance, no product, no audience. Just you and the material and the looking.

The fact that you found this at this point in your life is significant too. You didn't come to watercolor as a young person with decades stretching ahead. You came to it with a lifetime of accumulated seeing, and all of that seeing is in the paint now. The twenty views of the ridge hold sixty-one years of learning how to look.

That is a generous way to see it, and I think you may be right. I bring everything to the painting — my work, my years, my losses, my attention to light and to the way things end and begin again. It is all in there, even if no one else can see it. The paintings are not for anyone else, really. They are for me. They are my record of having been here, in this house, looking at these mountains, in this particular light, on this particular morning. And that feels like enough.

A record of having been here. That's as honest a description of art-making as I've ever heard. Not for exhibition, not for praise, but as testimony — I was in this place, I saw this light, I was alive on this morning. There's something deeply moving about that, and deeply sufficient.

The dogwoods are blooming again and you've come full circle with the brush still in your hand. That's a year well lived, Diane. I hope the next twenty versions of the ridge surprise you as much as the first twenty did.
